text: modify the lid so that it has two ports for pipes to pass through. have a coil of copper pipe inside the drum/bell area and pump water through it. you need a temp/pressure relief valve in case it boils, obviously. or, put the ports on the bottom and put the water reservoir below the rocket stove. that way, a thermostat can turn off the pump when it gets too hot, allowing the water to drain out of the pipe, preventing boil. you have to make sure to not start pumping again until it cools off, though. otherwise it will flash into steam when you start pumping into the hot pipe.

author: @BenEehayeh
text:
You are making Wood Gas.
The temperature when you begin to produce Wood Gas starts, if I remember correctly, at 250° C.
In an enclosed system, the top of the drum near the top of the Chimney, becomes the Wood Gas igniter.
Surrounding the stove with Cob is for thermal storage, not insulation. Metal dissipates heat at a very fast rate. Cob will hold onto the heat until the temperature of the stove is lower than the temperature of the Cob,at which point the Cob will radiate the stored heat for several hours after the fire has gone out.

text: I'm working on a bell type heat exchanger to go above my wood stove. With all the great comments this seems like a good forum to ask the question.. does anyone have experience with a double barrel type heat exchanger? I would have a 6-in insulated heat Riser coming out of the top of my wood stove covered by a barrel that's maybe 18 in diameter. The flue gas would spill over the heat riser and hit the top of the barrel in typical fashion then as the gas is spill down instead of directing them immediately to an exhaust pipe they would be traveling back up through a larger diameter outer Barrel. The exhaust pipe would be tapped in to the top of that outer Barrel. This would create in essence an inline heat exchanger. Any design thoughts?

author: @david-breitenfeld
text:
I have the same rocket stove and have added modifications to improve fire burn and heat output. The idea is to keep the hot exhaust gases in the big chamber longer to extract more heat before exiting. The heart of a rocket stove is heating up the wood and gases with a high temperature for a clean and smokeless burn and no creosote, thus always making sure that the modifications do not impair the burn by keeping a good air and burn mixture and flow to keep a clean and efficient burn. Disclaimer: modification voids warranty, so I know this but still want to experiment if it can help improve. 😊

My set up 
Colorado, USA, 8,500 (2590 m). Air/oxygen is thinner, keep in mind for adjusting burn. Air intake is from the room as warmer air helps efficient burn. House not the best sealed as built in 1954 with original windows and doors. 

The stove has 6”(15 cm) exhaust port, I took 6in (15cm) single wall stove pipe cut extension 8in (20 cm) long, then 6in elbow, then a 6in x4in reducer connected to 4in (10 cm) double wall pellet stove pipe for flue exhaust 16’ (4.8 m) rise, (note: my pipe is all hard double wall with stainless steel inner pipe and steel outside pipe instead of the flex pipe,) with a vacu-stack chimney cap (looks like a bow-tie ish thing) to help prevent wind back drafts. I have the pipe running up the old chimney and the pipe is secured with chain. The chimney is insulated around the pipe with mineral wool insulation. The 4” pipe creates good up draft.

Modifications 
1. used furnace cement to create a vortex inside the burn chamber so the flame swirls around the riser tube. Inside the J-tube at the 90 degree bend I placed some cement in the corners to help smooth out the flame swirl. On one side of the bottom horizontal burn tube, I created a wedge to direct the flame to one side to start the flame swirling.  (Note: you see this flame vortex on YouTube videos. That is what I am recreating).
2. I placed a piece of sheet metal to cover up half the 6” exhaust port (area: 28 sq in/ 180 sq cm) to 14 sq in/90 sq cm area. ( 4in area is 12 sq in/77 sq cm). A half circle sheet metal laid horizontally at the bottom above the exhaust port and blocked off one side so the exhaust flows to one side.  
3. placed metal caps on the side air intake ports so air only flows from the top.
The top of the stove gets 500F (260 c) and the 6 in exhaust pipe 170F (77 c), my goal is the exhaust to be around 100F (38C). 
The pellet hooper would create a steadier and continuous burn to get accurate temp readings. 
I wish a had a good thermo imaging camera to see the heat displacement. 

Next modifications
1. add sheet metal inside big cylinder to create a “screw type slide” for a counter clockwise exhaust flow to heat the sides longer and cools down more before existing stove. 
2. at the bottom I will have ¾ of the area with sheet metal so the exhaust will flow clockwise below it to exist.  
3. maybe small pipe in J-tube to the back bend to add hot air as a secondary burn

Wish list for manufacture 
1. Make the wood feeder tube 6” so the wood doesn’t get stuck 
2. Have a damper on top of the feeder tube to help reduce the air draft so it is not rushing too fast. The damper minimum closer is still bigger that the 4” exhaust pipe area.
3. have a reducer cap to place inside the exhaust port to have area 4” or 4 ½”

author: @hubrisnaut
text: When I see these rocket stoves I think of wood gasifiers. There could be some crossover in the designs that would be beneficial. For instance, I've seen some clever ways wood gasifiers deal with ash cleanout. Also, I think of efficient heat extraction from the exhaust gases. I had an "airtight" woodstove with a bimetal damper control. I came across a woodstove flue heat "reclaimer" (the unit was called Magic Heat, a box with transverse tubes to the gas stream and a blower.). The heat output went up est. 30-40 percent. The downside was there was a greater buildup of 'creosote' in the box and chimney from un-combusted gases which was the fault of the stove itself. The unit had a hand plunger actuated internal perforated plate that would scrap the buildup off the tubes back down into the woodstove. I had a friend that installed a new oil burner. It used a horizontal spiral heat exchange exhaust chamber to remove the heat. The exhaust gas temperature out was minimal. You could put your hand on the flue where it exited the building and it was just slightly warm.

author: @WetVidz
text:
i disagree with you Robert.  i do believe the entire burn chamber and chimney should be insulated to keep in as much heat as possible.
where people go wrong is having exposed steel in the burn chamber.
so if the inside is lined with ceramic sheet or if the burn chamber is not made of steel at all but rather clay/cement, then you can easily contain ~2k c. especially if you can surround this chamber with some mass.
where this high temperature in the burn chamber becomes useful is that you can burn less than substantial (almost green) wood.

the inner tube is not the heater, you do not want to extract heat from it.. the heat is extracted from the outer barrel and any subsequent flue or mass.

the awesome thing about the heat extraction barrel is that because the gasses flow downwards, and on the outside the convection causes an up flow of air, you get maximum heat transfer to the air.  (just like the blood flow in the leg of a seagull)

text:
If it's already burning at 99% plus efficiency there must be enough oxygen getting to the riser tube, so adding more air would probably be counter productive - just cooling the burn down. 
The thing I see lacking in this otherwise fantastic stove is some kind opf control over burn rate. This is probably not an issue if you are burning sticks in it because you can just feed more or less wood in as required, but if you are burning pellets, it would appear to me that it will just run at full chat all the while until the pellets run out. This may not matter if you are using it to heat a mass, but otherwise it would seem an oversight.

replies.author: @bli3366
replies.text:
The 'down tube' pellet feed tube that comes out of the collar on the bottom of the hopper can be adjusted to compensate for fuel rate. 

It is essentially adjusting the space between the bottom of the pellet hopper and the top of the burn grate, which is similar to adjusting the jets in the venturis of a carburetor. It requires the stove to not be in operation, but it is gravity-fed, so there isn't much that can be done about it. At least it works when the power is out.

author: @ProjectHighlander
text: Howdy. Love the videos, many thanks. I have been working with rocket heaters for many years and, Pls don't take offense at this but you are kind of missing the point. The J tube/burn chamber is only half of the equation. You have mastered this part wonderfully but there is so much more to the story. The liberator is closer to a true rocket stove as it includes the outer shell. The insulation in the riser is not just there to allow the temperature to remain at flash point, it is there to create a temperature difference between the riser and the space between the riser and the outer shell thus creating a huge push /suction/draw propelling the heat out of the system and, ideally, through an exhaust which, because of the power of the draw, can even be horizontal rather than vertical and be channelled through a heat sink like, for instance in my old  greenhouse rocket stove underfloor heating ( 20m of horizontal metal ducting underneath the grow-beds), or heated cob benches. but keep up the good work!

author: @williamgrimberg2510
text:
In the past I’ve worked on a vortex rocket stove which made the gravity fed flame concentrate into a swirl coming out from the stack which generate more heat while burning less fuel .
The idea is to have the gravity fed material drop down into a circular burn chamber slanted slightly upward to the round stack while positioning the air intake at just the right placement to control the adjustable draft or air intake .Burnt material or ash from pellets would drop down through a grate into a collection box . When built right and depending on the controlling of the air intake , you will actually have a very strong swirl of flame which is very interesting to see .

author: @robgad2271
text:
Robert: Rather than an ash pan under the amplifier elbow where you have a row of holes drilled, try an angled slot ground into the elbow on a 45° angle where the bottom of this air intake slot is 30mm from the bottom, no ash will roll out here and the relocation of the intake of the air jet air intake will enhance the draw toward through and up the flue.  Holes across the bottom will tend to create a burn there and not enhance the draw where it is needed.

I would like to see this in oil burner with an automatic light system for a thermostat and flame sensor, etc.  I also don't see a circulation blower on the hot air plenum, as a unit this size should have a better way to utilize flow to the space it is intended to heat.
 
And for thoughts and giggles, I am starting a build of a soybean (aka rapeseed oil) forced air furnace to replace my wood burning stove because the price of cut wood deliveries has gone out of sight in my are, and I assume it has gone up drastically in most places.

author: @chronosferatu345
text: I watched some videos on rocket stoves that used a long pipe connected to the stove that zig zagged from ceiling to floor placed next to a heat sink wall of stone or clay. This was done to use as much heat as possible in the home and not let the energy go to waste by venting it outside. After the heat from the pipe was spent, the pipe coiled back around the initial "heating drum" from the floor to heat up the air a little bit to help push what was used back outside since the air in the pipes had cooled and most of the energy was spent heating the home and/or heat sink. These folks channeled air from outside the house to feed a sealable chamber under the fire. I think the fire was on a grate of some kind. If the initial air for the fire in the lower chamber and air for the additional vents, like the holes made to help combust the gasified material, came from outside the house it may not be necessary to ventilate the home with a cracked window or whatever is used to ventilate the house. The air inside the home would only be warming the air inside and any gases that may cause harm wouldn't be able to enter the home and would return outside through whatever piping originally vents to the outside. The intake vents would need to be covered and away from the exhaust to keep from circulating non combustible air. I think much of this has already been done in small scale and in some homes. But I'm not sure that anyone has tried using air from outside the home to help combust the gasified material they are using for the rocket stove.

author: @topspeed250k5
text:
I'm a bit unhappy with the insulation positioning explanation.
If the wood burns at about 300-400° then it must be combustion of *some* of the pyrolysed gases, that raise the temperature up to 900°. This combustion must commence at the fire & progress from there. Much as you see gases from timber burning in an ordinary open fire.
So, to retain this heat of combustion, it makes sense to insulate the stove from after the firebox, to the start of the chimney, introduce the secondary air, &allow it to burn & radiate heat from there.
The way Rob explained it, it sounds as if the insulation is adding heat & raising the temp to 900° which of course is impossible & not what he means.
It's just that it doesn't make sense to allow any heat to escape immediately after the firebox. This is where the temperature is increasing up to the flashpoint of the main mass of gases. It seems to me that this should be insulated, not just the chimney.

author: @terryharris3393
text: It amazing that when you are given a properly designed rocket stove with an insulated riser and a heat exchange barrel that turns the exhaust gas 180 degrees you still fail to understand their importance and function and cling to your misunderstanding of how these impact the burn efficiency and draft capability beyond a simple  burn tube. The significantly increased BURN temperature due to the insulation and the increased retention time due to the vortex created by the 180 degree exhaust redirection are exactly why Liberator can make their claims of efficiency. PERIOD! The fortuitous location of the exhaust duct at ground level and the strong draft because of how hot the exhaust is have the ability to push the gas through a very long distance of serpentine ducting to heat a thermal mass. Hence the term rocket mass heater. This rocket stove is exactly Ianto Evans’ design with the exception that the materials of construction are masonry with the exception of the heat exchange barrel and exit ducting or manifold.

author: @DFPercush
text:
That looks very well put together. But if they're asking for ideas...
1) Another air channel beside or under the main intake to supply hot, fresh air to the flue/chimney
2) Crinkle the sides of the barrel to increase the surface area and radiative emissions. Some kind of wave pattern or 90 degree bends, like a washboard. It would use extra material for the same diameter, but that's kind of the point.
3) An option for running a coil of pipe through the barrel to heat water. Maybe just an access port on the top and bottom, with a pipe fitting of some standard size, would be useful enough to allow people to mod that themselves without taking an angle grinder to their beautiful stove.

4) A thermostat. Some kind of feedback loop for output temperature vs. intake air. I'm thinking the effluent temperature from #3 here, but perhaps there could be a design for the outside air temperature as well. I'm not sure how much you can throttle this thing down without affecting the burn quality, smoke, etc, but it seems like some kind of thermostat would be very useful. It might be possible with a bi-metallic strip, a lever to increase the travel distance, and a bicycle brake cable. The valve would need to be easy to move if you're relying purely on the mechanical force of the strip. A coil of something with a high coefficient of thermal expansion might also work.

author: @hunnybunnysheavymetalmusic6542
text:
I'd just use a shop vac with a decent length of hose to remove the ash [but only when it has cooled, naturally].

The only other thing I could possibly suggest, for how little I can see it from a distance, is putting radiator fins around the outside.

In fact, you could literally add them completely independent of the actual body of the unit with a couple of wide pieces of shipping banding [it is typically a hardened steel], and if you can get a couple feet/meters of it, one for the top and one for the bottom, OR some really large hose clamps [I've seen some amazingly large ones out there, but you might still need to daisy-chain 2 or 3 to get around your barrel], take some simple L or U or even square aluminum extrude, cut it to the length of the barrel, cut 2 slits in the aluminum to poke your metal strapping through, and then wrap the whole thing like a beach fence around the barrel so that at least one whole flat side contacts the barrel so it can conduct some heat, and let the other side(s) facing out into the room go into convection and radiation mode, as Aluminum will do a really good job of extracting a lot of that heat and liberating it into the surrounding area.

To close up the ends of packing banding [by the way, some times you can find the stuff thrown away any place a lot of large shipping pallets can be found, so if you look in the alleys of some industrial parks, you might find strips of it for free, plenty large  enough for the job] just take a small drill bit or, if it proves too hard, get a carbide tipped burr and put it on a dremel motor, make yourself 2 matching holes close to the end, bend them over 90 degrees with a pair of pliers, so you can slip a bolt and a nut through them and gently tighten the straps just enough to do the task without breaking them.

These can be put on and taken off, they can be cleaned behind, you COULD, should you be so obsessed with efficiency, strip away the paint and apply thermal paste between the bare metal and the bare aluminum, but I get a feeling that would be a real waste of time...

The  only reason I might ever bother taking the radiator fins off is to clean them, or clean behind them. Not sure it would be with the effort to clean them much more than sweeping them occasionally with a fuller's brush for dust and cobwebs anyhow.

author: @sinenomine9093
text: Instead of an ash pan, provide an ash hoe with which to reach in through the fire chamber & rake the ashes out.  In order to introduce secondary oxygen to the vertical rocket tube, drill a 1" hole through the vertical fire tube about 5" up from the bottom & tap it for 1" pipe thread,  drill a second hole through the outside barrel to correspond with the first, making it just large enough to slide a piece of threaded 1" black iron pipe through the outer barrel & screw it into the fire tube.  At the end of the 1" pipe sticking out of the barrel, drill a series of holes (1/2" dia).  1" schedule 40 steel should have an inside diameter of 1.049 in., and the outside diameter of 3/4" sch 40 steel pipe should be about 1,050 in,. Take a short length of 3/4 " pipe and drill holes corresponding to those in the 1" pipe and then turn it on a lath until it just fits into the 1" pipe, & stop one end.  To adjust the secondary air flow, rotate & slide the inner tube to expose more or fewer holes through the outer pipe.

author: @tskogen6905
text: So much talk about rocket stoves while excluding the mass. With a rocket mass stove the first chimney is to act as a pump that can push the hot gasses a great distance through a mass before being exhausted. On larger models the push is strong enough to travel 40-60". The lower the chimney temp the more efficient. I have heard of some as low as 70-100F. If you want to regulate it to not burn too hot I have thought that limiting the air available in the main tube might be helpful. I really like your vortex model and think that as others have suggested that the incoming air should come down the top of the main tube. You may need to regulate the amount of air allowed to travel this way also, to limit cooling. If you welded a plate near the bottom of vertical pipe at the top of the vortex generator and sealed to the outer barrel. The exhaust air would have to be released through the side wall above vortex separator plate. Then see how far the exhaust gasses could be pushed. I understand that smaller 4" stoves don't have as much push. I would like to experiment with a taller primary chimney to see if you could increase the push. I really appreciate you and your channel. I am looking forward to seeing what you will do next!

author: @EumlOriginal
text:
I've been dealing with this topic for a long time and see some potential for improvement

As you said, a separate air supply is a definite improvement, I add that this air should already be preheated if possible

I would design the combustion chamber like an inverted cyclone separator, so you get a circulating combustion chamber, which means that the heat is very centered and can also have an effect on the ash so that it can really burn completely and it would also ensure that the combustion chamber can clean itself or. you can collect the ashes

author: @MysticMCWizard
text:
Hi,
 yes I can think of a few suggestions.. you covered two in your vid with a secondary air inlet and an ash pan.. but the only issue you need to ensure is that after the initial burn phase that no air will escape from where its not supposed to.. being as it is meant for internal use you don't want any form of carbon monoxide to leak.. 

My suggestion includes a secondary burn feed from the main air inlet (Shared inlet) this will negate any blowback if it were to occur as it would be dragged back in via the initial burn inlet.
Ash extraction could be done by installing a service hatch on the side or bottom as you suggest, but this will need to be sealed using fire rope or caulking..

Air flow is like water and electricity, it always follows the path of least resistance, so although the heat is pumping out of the top of the rocket stove and hitting the top plate (which can suffer from heat damage over long term) most of the heat also flows down the side of the exhaust port.  My suggestion would be to insert some baffle plates to force the air in  circular motion around the radiant heater section before it gets to the exhaust port. Yes this might slow the air flow down a bit. But it would also make it more efficient in using the available heat produced to come into contact with the outer edge.

Also on that note, utilising the available heat on the outer edge .. my biggest suggestion is to add fins to the internal and external surfaces of the radian heat drum.. this will give a larger surface area for heat transfer and also increase the rate at which the heat is dissipated out of the system..


Only problem is.. now I've made these suggestions on a public forum they cannot be used in a patent and are open to the public..  that sort of thing really sucks if your trying to make the ideas novel and unique.. to improve your own patent applications.
Anyhow good luck to them on developing their product 😎 👍

author: @salimufari
text: The idea behind Insulating the Rocket Stove is not to do anything in the way of heat radiation. It's 2 purposes are to First get the burn box area up to temperature ASAP. Second is insulating the Inner wall is to Protect the steel from being destroyed by the excessive heat & keeping enough heat in the riser tube is to make sure the wood gas ignites before it leaves said tube. This is where the secondary air comes into play right at the vertical turn of the riser tube.

author: @artytomparis
text:
That tank isn't just a radiator. The gases are vented from the base which allows them to travel horizontally through a mass storage chimney, where the heat can radiate outwards and upwards into the room or space which the chimney passes through. The 'mass storage' part of the chimney is often clay or brick and is sometimes built up as a seating or sleeping surface arrangement. The gasses which escape into the outside of the building are considerably cooler than normal for a chimney and contain few pollutants. 
However! If the chimney isn't built properly and the gasses don't combust completely in the fireplace there can be a condensation of turpentine liquid and bitumen at different locations in the chimney, depending on the temperature and drainage spots should be incorporated.

author: @allanwood3562
text:
I imagine that the Liberator is a pellet stove which is great for fuel consistency if the pellets can be metered in with some control. When I was was in the solid fuel heater testing game we judged efficiency by taking into account the measured energy value of the fuel and seeing how much of that could be delivered to the calorimetry room. You do get flue losses ultimately as is only to be expected.
Pellet fuel can be fairly expensive but the units were generally very efficient. The quoted 99.5% of the Liberator might be a bit optimistic however although pellet stoves could have low flue exhaust temps which demonstrated greater energy was distributed to the room.

replies.author: @bli3366
replies.text:
The pellet feed tube inserted into the pellet hopper can be adjusted for 'fuel control' kind of like adjusting the jets on a carburetor can do the same. 
That 99.5% is EPA -tested "combustion efficiency"; the HHV values on tests are from 68% to 78% or something around there.

replies.author: @topspeed250k5
replies.text:
@jamesross1003 thanks!
I came to the opposite conclusion lol.
An abundance of O2 is needed to ensure the complete combustion of pyrolysed gases. There can't be an over-abundance, any shortage will cause CO and not CO2.
Yes it will get hotter but controlling the temp by restricting the airflow is not the thing to do at all.
Looking at the size & number of holes in Rob's stove, I don't think a single tube would have the same total cross-section. So probably, as well as the "ashpan handle" it would need a couple more tubes in the upper corners of the stove body.

replies.author: @jamesross1003
replies.text: @topspeed250k5 The drop pan I spoke of would not change the air intake at all.  We already know from the manufacturers build that it will work at 99.5% efficiency and will not over heat.  Those gases are already being burned very well.  My idea for the drop pan was only for convenience and ease of operation rather than efficiency to make it more palatable to the consumer.  It is a fine balance which is why I said it may get too hot and that any changes would require a lot of testing.  If as is it works as well as claimed, an additional air intake with a baffle may or may not work.  It is unknown until testing and that includes any ideas anyone throws out there including me, you, and everyone else.  Reread my comment, I was not criticizing you or your ideas.  I just said it may get too hot and would need testing.  If it does, it melts down or causes other issues like an external fire.  I have built pulse jets/rockets before and have found that they can get hot enough to melt down.  Thanks for the input!

replies.author: @jamesross1003
replies.text: @topspeed250k5 I guess what I am trying to say is that yes there can be an overabundance of air as it would get too hot.  You would have a forge capable of melting metal.  You want to hit that sweet spot of being hot enough, but not too hot.  The design as it already is hits that sweet spot.  I feel we won't really get anywhere trying to make it produce even more heat.  I also feel any design changes would need a lot of testing to make sure it stays within that sweet spot.  Also, said design changes would need to be practical, cheap, easy to do, and be something that potential consumers would want hence convenience driven.  Just my 2 cents worth, but if we add enough of those 2 cents worth we might end up with some decent amount of change if you get my meaning.

replies.author: @gan314159
replies.text: the 'expansion chamber' is known as a bell typically in rocket stoves, and you're correct - its there to extract heat rapidly into the room and the cooling helps the draw into the burn chamber. the downside being that if the air in the bell and flue is too cold it can prevent the rocket effect from starting, and in the worse examples has resulted in the fire going the other way and into the room, with dire consequences.

author: @richardsandwell2285
text: My Dunsley Yorkshire stove could almost be described as a kind of rocket stove, it has a large fire box for logs, at the back and at the bottom of the chamber is a slot maybe 11/2" high, it is there where I have to light it. The firebox merely undergoes Pyrolysis, once the flames exit through the slot in the back they go through what would be best described has a venturi chamber, this is firebrick insulated and a special brick with I think about 8 holes in it which line up with holes in the back of the steel. The flames drag in more air, and the afterburn chamber goes to about 1500 degrees where it burns off all the smoke, this is then sent around a boiler jacket before heading for the chimney at the same time it passes over the top of the fire box fire brick which provides more heat for pyrolysis. It burns very clean provides hot water and central heating in the worst of winter, it has just not been cold enough yet to use it. But the design could be used in a rocket stove and you may be able to get a diagram online. Another thing I once read about was a professor hills boiler, these involve a down burn feed and a super insulated refractory channel with a air blower.

author: @totherarf
text:
Now That is a stove and a half!
Now I do think you need to be careful what you wish for, especially when asking a guy like me for ideas! 
My head is full of ideas and bumfluf. So I'll let you decide if it is bumfluf or idea, and if idea weather it is useful or not! :o)

As I understand it the insulation in the riser is primarily to stop oxidation of the steel at too high a temp ... so is a good thing. It has the advantage of letting the gasses get hotter and therefore increasing the draught, which if you are going to suck the heat out later in the process is a great thing! I have seen ceramics used for the riser because of this reason. A possibility might be to ship with a ceramic duct? Obviously this could be a problem in regards to damage so might need a good coat of looking at (possibly removable foam inserts?) Also this could be a good revenue stream it terms of replacements a few years down the line?

You did not show it but is there a separate air intake to the post burn flame section from th front? If so your extra holes might not be needed. One aspect not talked about too much is external venting. By this I mean having an air source taken from outside so you are not burning the room air and chucking that warmed up air out of the chimney. This can be via an air brick and duct but would need an alteration to the stove to accept this! This might go a long way to making larger stoves UK compliant with regs (I think over 5Kw needs an external vent)
The exhaust point is from the bottom of the heater? This could go to a flue or to a thermal mass heater? Maybe a sand box with a solid flue going the length of it (and back) If it were made of an existing flue materiel it should be compliant rather than the homemade
 versions! 
It might be possible to construct the body of the stove larger but thinner and have masonry on the inside to at as thermal mass? 
It might also be possable to construct the stove with a water jacket over part of it  to act as a secondary circuit water heater (like those coal back boilers we all used to have in grandad's age!

Final idea (for now) How about running a pipe through the body of the stove. Connected to a cheap low volume silent fan this would force air through to be heated and blown out from the heater without being contaminated by fumes. The fan may need to be fitted on the end of a pipe to keep it remote for noise and cooling.

author: @patri0t1776
text:
Dear Robert,
  I think you misunderstand the point of insulation on the riser (stack, "chimney"). The purpose is to increase the riser heat inside therefore completing the burn so no violatiles are left to exit the system. By the time it hits the top of the barrel (where the final combustion of CO, fine particulates & any other remaining volatiles usually happens) combustion finishes. The riser is not for heat output/efficiency per se but for combustion efficiency, completeness and "cleanliness" of exhaust gas. The barrel top and sides are the outlets for heat radiation, not the riser.
There's another important reason for insulating the riser. A thermal differential is needed to facilitate continued movement of flue gases. Maintained heat going up at a high rate, cooler gases to pull down at a high rate. If the riser was not insulated the heat inside vs outside would be very close to equal, stunting proper system operation.
That insulative effect is necessary regardless of build material choice.  Preventing rapid steel corrosion and destruction is actually a beneficial side effect, not a primary function. 
  A well designed rocket heater/ rocket mass heater (the Liberator should be attached to a thermal mass, though not necessary) should output only CO2 & H2O while using minimal fuel & extracting nearly every btu of heat available. That's the goal anyhow. 
  Not trying to be overly critical, just adding to the conversation😊. I watch nearly all your videos and I'm a very big fan. 👍

author: @TheShakke100
text:
Hi Rob. You’re absolutely right about chimney insulation for a ROCKET STOVE (though chimney height is clearly important …HOWEVER, for a ROCKET MASS HEATER (like that liberator clearly is), the chimney is typically made completely out of firebrick for insulation! This is to MAXIMISE THE HEAT FOR CONVECTION. For instance, my rocket mass heater, built 3 yrs ago (from hundreds of YouTube videos AND the purchase of The Rocket Mass Heater Builder’s guide (Ernie Wisner), EVEN THE GAP of almost precisely 45mm (for 6 inch design) must be maintained. This allows the ‘draw’ to not only continue down and out the barrel, but ‘snake’ along the wall up to 15 METERS (covered in thermal mass). 
I have pictures I took, but no idea how to insert them into comments :(
You’re AWESOME! Keep up the amazing work!! You’re helping more people in the world that I’m sure you could possibly imagine!!
Cheers,
Mark Caudrey, Montreal, Canadian chap ;)

author: @tichrontus
text:
I'll check their website and see if a cross-section diagram is avai before making suggestions. But i notice there are some confusions with the terms used.
Flash point - Temperature where a liquid fuel releases enough vapours to maintain self sustaining combustion. It must not be confused with temperature a fuel ignites. You can heat a fuel with oxygen to its flash point but without ignition, nothing happens.
Autoignition temperature - This is the temperature fuel actually ignites in the presence of oxygen and it's well above the flash point for the same fuel. This will be the temperature you wouldn't want to drop below at the region where you want combustion to take place, especially with a 2nd stage air intake if you decided to add one.
Flame temperature - This is the general temperature at very close proximity of where the combustion takes place. However, it varies greatly based on air composition, intake air temperature, part of the flame you're measuring, type of fuel used, types and amount of contaminations in the fuel and so on.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yeah - I am trying to pass a concept here mate - so I can playa little fast and loose with terms - my bad lol

replies.author: @tichrontus
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering No worries mate, it can be just extra reading for those interested.
I took a look at their website, no cross-section though. But various angles from their video gave me a pretty clear picture. Given that a rocket stove's main feature is in its simplicity, there's only one possible improvement i can think of. Most energy lost is through the exhaust. So they can run a pipe just over the exhaust as a heat exchanger, down and feed into the point just before the flames end. This recovers some of the heat to be used as preheated secondary air intake. That should bring up the actual efficiency for useful heating (which i know for sure is not at 99.5% =P).

author: @andrewallason4530
text: To allow the point for air entry and easy cleaning, try a 50x100mm (2x4inch) pipe under the bottom of the ‘J’, coming to the ‘front’ of the unit, with an removable ash drawer.  The drawer can then also be opened and closed by varying amounts to regulate the air then entering the bottom of the flue. That way all three intakes (main air intake, fuel hopper and ‘bypass’ air intake with the ash collector drawer) are at the ‘front’ of the heater.

replies.author: @ogi22
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering I have seen a few variations of "afterburner" and so far one was very appealing to me. You just drilled some holes in the bottom of the chimney. Air that is drawn this way is at room temperature, so about 20 degrees C. As you obviously know that, in all substances it will try to reach thermal equilibrium, so you are wasting energy here, cooling that part which should be the hottest.
In a few designs i saw that this air was supplied by a square profile, cut off one side then welded along the hearth. One guy even made holes along the chimney pipe and just drilled them on an angle, which gave the exhaust gasses a vortex effect.
Anyways, that's my two cents on this topic :)
P.S. I really love that someone reached out to you with such request. It means you are doing a wonderful job and it's getting noticed :)

replies.author: @topspeed250k5
replies.text:
I agree that drilling holes at the chimney base for secondary air intake is not the best idea. The air should come via a tube from the front "mouth" of the stove, so it's preheated in the fire section. Turn the chimney end of the tube upwards and weld it positioned in  the middle of the ash pan.
Then the air intake tube (maybe 3/4 to an inch?) is also the long handle of the ash pan. Pull it out & empty

The ventilator holes shouldn't leak ash, since they are drawing air. But the easiest fix is just a short angled up tube on the outside. 
More on those holes, if you put one near each of the three available corners, they would add oxygen while starting the vortex. You don't need a lot to start that, just a hint (also keeps the temp from dropping), which three maybe 3/8 inch holes would do.

replies.author: @Techno_Nomadic
replies.text: Essentially what a "rocket mass heater" is. A large earthen mass which the stove pipe exits through. Usually a long, low couch sized lump of cobb which the stove pipe is embedded in the bottom of. You run the rocket hard for a couple hours, which heats the cobb, which after the fire is out and the air intake blocked off and dampers closed, continues to radiate heat.
